objections.description: The artifact is truncated mid-token. The [data-theme='light'] block is cut off at `--skeleton-base: #e4e4e7; /* surface` — skeleton-shine, status colors for light mode, shadow overrides for light mode (dark rgba values will look absurdly heavy on white backgrounds), and the closing brace are all missing. This means the delivered token system is literally uncompilable. Shipping this means every light-mode surface has dark-mode shadows (0,0,0,0.3-0.5 opacity on white — ugly black halos), status colors designed for dark backgrounds render on light without adjustment (emerald-400/amber-400 have poor contrast on white), and skeleton shimmer is broken.
objections.suggested_fix: Complete the [data-theme='light'] block with: --skeleton-shine, all --status-* overrides for light (e.g., emerald-600, red-600, amber-600, blue-600 for AA compliance on white), --shadow-sm/md/lg/sheet/focus with reduced opacity (rgba(0,0,0,0.06) through rgba(0,0,0,0.12)), --border-accent override, and the closing brace. Every token that references a color must have a light-mode counterpart or an explicit comment stating it passes contrast checks in both modes.

objections.description: The spec says `#/search?q={encodeURIComponent(preset.query)}` but hash-based routing means the query string is INSIDE the hash fragment. The browser's `location.search` will be empty — `location.hash` will contain everything. The navigation.jsx parser must split `location.hash` on `?` to extract query params. This is not documented anywhere. If someone implements useRoute() by reading `new URL(location).searchParams`, every preset-initiated search will silently fail with an empty query. This is a guaranteed bug.
objections.suggested_fix: In Section 3 under routing, add an explicit implementation note: 'Hash query params are NOT accessible via location.search. The Router must parse location.hash by splitting on the first `?` character: `const [path, qs] = location.hash.slice(1).split('?', 2); const query = Object.fromEntries(new URLSearchParams(qs || ''));` — include this exact parsing logic in the spec to prevent misimplementation.

objections.description: `AbortController.timeout(8000)` is a static method introduced in Node 17.3+ and available in browsers only since Chrome 103 / Safari 16 / Firefox 100. Your browser support floor is Safari 15 and Chrome 89. This API does not exist on those browsers. The timeout will silently not work — no abort, no error, requests hang forever on slow connections.
objections.suggested_fix: Replace AbortController.timeout() with a manual timeout pattern: create a standard AbortController, start a setTimeout(8000) that calls controller.abort(), and clear the timeout on response/error. This works on every browser in the support matrix. Document in useFetch implementation notes.

objections.description: StatusCard polls every 30 seconds with aria-live='polite', but the spec doesn't define what happens when the component unmounts mid-poll. If the poll's setInterval fires after unmount, it'll attempt a state update on an unmounted component. Additionally, if the user switches tabs (document.hidden), polling should pause — burning API calls and battery while the tab is backgrounded is unacceptable on mobile.
objections.suggested_fix: Add to Section 6 or Section 4: 'StatusCard polling uses setInterval inside useEffect with cleanup. Polling MUST pause when document.visibilityState === "hidden" via a visibilitychange listener. On visibility restore, fire an immediate fetch then resume interval. The useEffect cleanup function clears both the interval and the visibility listener.'

objections.description: The esbuild command uses `--target=es2020` but the browser support floor includes Safari 15 which shipped Dec 2021. ES2020 features like optional chaining and nullish coalescing are fine on Safari 15, but if any authored code uses ES2022 features (top-level await, Array.at(), Object.hasOwn(), class static blocks), esbuild's es2020 target will NOT downlevel all of them — esbuild doesn't polyfill built-in methods like Array.at(). The target protects syntax but not API surface.
objections.suggested_fix: Add a note under Build System: 'esbuild --target=es2020 downlevels SYNTAX only, not runtime APIs. Do not use Array.prototype.at(), Object.hasOwn(), structuredClone(), or other post-ES2020 built-in methods without checking browser support. Add this as a lint rule or PR checklist item.' Alternatively, set --target=safari15,chrome89,firefox90 for more precise output.

objections.description: The health check gates ALL screen-specific fetches. If /api/health returns a transient 503 but the rest of the API is functional (common during rolling deploys where health checks fail before routes), the entire app shows a dead screen. A single flaky health endpoint should not be a total availability blocker.
objections.suggested_fix: Change health check behavior to: on failure, show a non-blocking warning banner at the top of Shell ('Server connectivity issue — some features may be unavailable') and proceed to mount screens with their own fetch cycles. Only show the full-screen error state if BOTH health check AND the first screen-specific fetch fail. This provides graceful degradation instead of a binary kill switch.

objections.location: Section 7 — tokens.css / --text-tertiary contrast in dark mode
objections.description: The comment claims --text-tertiary (#71717a / zinc-500) has 4.6:1 contrast on --surface-0 (#09090b). Actual calculated contrast ratio of #71717a on #09090b is approximately 4.57:1 — this technically passes AA for normal text (≥4.5:1) but is razor-thin margin. Any slight color shift from font rendering, subpixel antialiasing, or screen calibration could push it below threshold. More critically, --text-tertiary on --surface-1 (#18181b) drops to ~4.1:1, which fails AA. The spec uses --text-tertiary for tab labels and secondary info — these WILL appear on surface-1 cards.
objections.suggested_fix: Bump dark-mode --text-tertiary to #7e7e88 or #82828c (targeting ≥5.0:1 on surface-0 and ≥4.5:1 on surface-1). Recalculate and document contrast ratios for EVERY surface pairing where tertiary text appears, not just surface-0.

objections.description: Inter font files listed as inter-regular.woff2, inter-medium.woff2, inter-semibold.woff2 but no @font-face declarations are specified anywhere in the artifact. tokens.css references the font-family but doesn't include the @font-face rules that map weight values to files. Without these, the browser will use the system fallback stack and Inter never loads.
objections.suggested_fix: Add @font-face declarations to tokens.css (or a dedicated fonts.css imported first) with explicit font-weight mappings: 400 → inter-regular.woff2, 500 → inter-medium.woff2, 600 → inter-semibold.woff2. Include font-display: swap in each declaration.

suggestions.description: Use esbuild's browser-list target format `--target=safari15,chrome89,firefox90` instead of `--target=es2020`. This is more precise, self-documenting of the actual support matrix, and lets esbuild make optimal decisions per-browser rather than per-spec-version.

The spec says 'WAI-ARIA tabs pattern' but bottom navigation bars are semantically `role=navigation` with links, not `role=tablist` with `role=tab`. The tablist pattern implies content panels switch without page navigation and requires aria-controls pointing to panel IDs. Since these are route-level navigations that change the URL, use `<nav aria-label="Main"> <a role="link">` with aria-current="page" on the active link. This matches WAI-ARIA Practices guidance for site navigation, not tab panels.

Add a `<SkipLink>` component as the very first child of Shell that becomes visible on focus: 'Skip to main content' linking to the main element's ID. This is a baseline WCAG 2.4.1 requirement that's missing entirely.

Add a retry strategy for retryable errors: single automatic retry with 1-second delay before surfacing the error to the user. This eliminates the majority of transient network blips without complex retry logic. The manual 'Retry' button in ErrorState remains as the user-initiated fallback.

Add `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` block that sets all --duration-* tokens to 0ms. The spec mentions no reduced-motion accommodation. Without this, users with vestibular disorders get all animations at full duration — a WCAG 2.3.3 failure.

Add Step M3.5: verify that web_server.py Content-Type headers for .js files are `application/javascript` (not `text/plain`). Some simple Python HTTP servers serve .js with wrong MIME type, which causes browsers to refuse module execution with a strict MIME type error. This is a silent, hard-to-diagnose failure.
